topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing my retinas. I stood frozen in the doorway, my breath catching in my throat, watching the canvas—my canvas—gaze back at me from the pedestal. It was a masterpiece of anguish, the very core of my recent struggles laid bare, and it was being admired by a crowd I didn't recognize, all of them nodding with an air of profound, unsettling appreciation. My hands were clammy, slick against the fabric of my worn jacket. A strange, cold pressure built behind my ribs, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I wanted to bolt, to vanish into the shadows, but my feet were rooted to the polished floor. A voice, smooth and condescending, drifted over the murmur of the onlookers, and I could only stare, a knot tightening painfully in my chest.

[story 2]

I crept around the corner of the corridor, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs. The air in the exhibition space was thick with hushed reverence, and every glance seemed directed toward the large, unfinished sculpture I had spent six months perfecting. When I finally reached the viewing area, the realization hit me like a physical shockwave. My work was there, hanging prominently, bathed in spotlights, and I wasn't the one in charge of this display. A tremor ran through my hands, causing me to grip my phone so tightly my knuckles turned white. I took a shaky step back, my eyes darting everywhere, searching for an escape route, any crack in the facade of this invasion. The silence in my own head was deafening, a hollow echo where my confidence used to reside.

[story 3]

"Excuse me," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy, barely audible above the low hum of conversation. I tried to approach the display, my movements jerky and unnatural. When a curator, whose expression was utterly devoid of warmth, turned to me, his gaze swept over the painting with an almost clinical detachment before settling on my face. There was a flicker in his eyes—a brief, assessing look that sent a jolt of pure alarm through me. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to recoil, to physically shrink away from the space. My mouth went dry, and I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a burning sensation that had nothing to do with the gallery’s temperature. I opened my mouth to protest, but no sound emerged; I was simply paralyzed, watching the intrusion unfold.
